original_hypothesis.title: Whitelist Bypass: Unwhitelisted Addresses Pass checkWhitelist Due to Default Mapping Value
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The checkWhitelist function uses the condition `_allowedWhitelistIndex == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex` to revert for non-whitelisted users. However, unwhitelisted addresses have a default _whitelistIndex of 0. When _allowedWhitelistIndex is set to any value > 0 (normal operation), the check `0 > N` evaluates to false, so the revert is never triggered for unwhitelisted addresses. This means ANY address can buy tokens from the Uniswap V3 pool during the whitelist period, completely nullifying the whitelist mechanism.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The logic flaw is in the boolean condition. The intended behavior is to allow only addresses with index 1..._allowedWhitelistIndex. The missing check is `_whitelistIndex[to] == 0` which would catch unwhitelisted addresses. This is a clear code defect observable from the source.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Default mapping value 0 for _whitelistIndex[to] bypasses `_whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex` check when _allowedWhitelistIndex > 0

preconditions: _allowedWhitelistIndex is set to a value > 0 (normal whitelist operation)
_locked is false (whitelist period active but not fully locked)
Transfer originates from the Uniswap pool (_pool)
Recipient is not the contract owner

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: _whitelistIndex[to] defaults to 0 for unwhitelisted addresses; 0 > N is always false for N > 0
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing check for _whitelistIndex[to] == 0 to catch unwhitelisted addresses

analysis_questions: Confirm that _whitelistIndex is only set via _addWhitelistedAddress and starts at 1
Verify no other code path sets _whitelistIndex for arbitrary addresses
Confirm that unwhitelisted addresses always have _whitelistIndex == 0
validation_requirements: Deploy Whitelist, set _allowedWhitelistIndex to 5, verify unwhitelisted address passes checkWhitelist
Verify whitelisted address with index 3 also passes (correct behavior)
Verify address with index 6 correctly reverts

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker buys VULT tokens from Uniswap V3 pool during whitelist period without being whitelisted. The _maxAddressCap check still applies but the whitelist gate is completely bypassed.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete whitelist bypass allows unlimited non-whitelisted addresses to purchase tokens, destroying fair launch mechanics
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All legitimate whitelisted participants who expected restricted access
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None during active period; damage is immediate

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for unwhitelisted address attempting purchase during active whitelist period

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring purchases from non-whitelisted addresses

validation_result.mechanism: default mapping value bypass in whitelist index check
validation_result.impact_summary: Any unwhitelisted address can buy tokens from the Uniswap V3 pool during the whitelist period because the default _whitelistIndex value of 0 passes the guard condition when _allowedWhitelistIndex > 0.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The checkWhitelist function in Whitelist.sol uses the condition `_allowedWhitelistIndex == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex` to revert for non-whitelisted users. Whitelisted addresses receive indices starting from 1 via `_addWhitelistedAddress`, which increments `_whitelistCount` and assigns it. Unwhitelisted addresses have a default `_whitelistIndex` of 0. When `_allowedWhitelistIndex` is set to any N > 0 (standard operation to progressively open the whitelist), the check for an unwhitelisted address evaluates to `(0 == 0) || (0 > N)` which is `false || false = false`, so the revert is never triggered. This means ANY address can pass the whitelist check, completely nullifying the whitelist mechanism. The correct logic should include an explicit check for `_whitelistIndex[to] == 0`.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The vulnerable code is at Whitelist.sol lines 152-154. The _addWhitelistedAddress function at lines 164-168 confirms that whitelisted indices start at 1 and unwhitelisted addresses retain the default value of 0. The condition at line 152 fails to account for the 0-index case.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When _allowedWhitelistIndex > 0 (normal operation), unwhitelisted addresses have _whitelistIndex[to] == 0 (default). The check `0 > N` evaluates to false, so the revert is never triggered for unwhitelisted addresses. The condition should also check `_whitelistIndex[to] == 0` to reject addresses that were never added to the whitelist.

This function confirms that whitelisted addresses receive indices starting from 1 (_whitelistCount is pre-incremented). Addresses never added to the whitelist retain the default mapping value of 0, which is the same value used to detect 'not yet whitelisted' in this function but is NOT properly checked in checkWhitelist.

calling_sequence: 1. User swaps ETH for VULT on Uniswap V3 pool, triggering VultisigWhitelisted._beforeTokenTransfer(from=_pool, to=buyer, amount)
3. checkWhitelist enters the `if (from == _pool && to != owner())` branch
4. _locked is false (whitelist period active but not fully locked) → passes
6. Whitelist index check: _allowedWhitelistIndex=5, _whitelistIndex[to]=0 (unwhitelisted) → condition is `(5==0 || 0>5)` = `(false || false)` = false → revert NOT triggered
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical flaw is at Whitelist.sol line 152. For an unwhitelisted address `to`, `_whitelistIndex[to]` returns the default Solidity mapping value of 0. The guard condition `_allowedWhitelistIndex == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex` evaluates to false when `_allowedWhitelistIndex > 0`, because `0 > N` is always false for positive N. Thus the revert is skipped and the unwhitelisted address passes all subsequent checks (oracle cap, contribution tracking) as if it were whitelisted.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for Whitelist.sol. The defect is in the Solidity logic itself, not configuration-dependent.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario: 1) Owner deploys Whitelist, adds 10 addresses to whitelist, sets _allowedWhitelistIndex=10 and _locked=false. 2) Any non-whitelisted address buys VULT from the Uniswap V3 pool. 3) VultisigWhitelisted._beforeTokenTransfer calls checkWhitelist. 4) The whitelist index check passes because _whitelistIndex[attacker]=0 and `0 > 10` is false. 5) The attacker successfully acquires tokens, subject only to the _maxAddressCap limit enforced by the oracle. This completely defeats the purpose of the whitelist, which was to restrict early access to approved addresses.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL: The whitelist mechanism is completely non-functional during the intended whitelist period. Any address (not just whitelisted ones) can purchase tokens from the Uniswap V3 pool, destroying fair launch mechanics. The only remaining protection is the per-address contribution cap (_maxAddressCap), but since anyone can participate, an unlimited number of Sybil addresses can drain the pool. Whitelisted participants who expected priority access receive no advantage.
recommended_mitigations: Add explicit check for _whitelistIndex[to] == 0: `if (_allowedWhitelistIndex == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex) { revert NotWhitelisted(); }`
Alternative: Change index assignment to start from 0 and use a separate boolean mapping or sentinel value for the unwhitelisted state
Add comprehensive unit tests that verify unwhitelisted addresses are rejected during the whitelist period
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Whitelist contract. 2. Set _pool, _vultisig, _oracle addresses. 3. Add 5 addresses to whitelist via addBatchWhitelist. 4. Set _allowedWhitelistIndex to 5 and _locked to false. 5. Call checkWhitelist(pool, unwhitelisted_address, amount) from the vultisig address. 6. Observe that the call succeeds without reverting, proving the whitelist bypass.
additional_testing_needed: Verify end-to-end with actual VultisigWhitelisted token and Uniswap V3 pool swap
